techniques.evidence_quote: pcTattletale's servers could be tricked into turning over the private keys for its Amazon Web Services account
pcTattletale's servers could be tricked into turning over the private keys for its Amazon Web Services account, which grants access to the spyware's operations
enjoy the loot: pctattle.tar.gz manufacture.tar.gz pctattletaleapidev.tar pctattle_app.sql.gz pctattletaleapi.tar ... pcttlivereaderprod-bucket-xp252tf4ezzo.tar pctts3logs.tar
The hacker posted a message on pcTattletale's website late Friday, claiming to have hacked the servers containing pcTattletale's operations.
pcTattletale's website briefly had links containing files from its servers, which appeared to include some victims' stolen data

attack_chain.description: The attacker sent a crafted unauthenticated SOAP request to pcTattletale's public-facing authentication endpoint (Authenticationv16.php/AddComputer), registering a fake device without running any spyware client. The server responded with sensitive data including AWS credentials embedded in the SOAP response.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: first, lets try to register without actually running the spyware... res = requests.post(url, headers=headers, data=soap_request)

attack_chain.technique_name: Unsecured Credentials: Cloud Instance Metadata API
attack_chain.description: The SOAP API response from pcTattletale's server returned plaintext AWS Access Key ID and Secret Access Key (root account credentials) directly in the XML body, exposing the full AWS root account.

The attacker used the exfiltrated AWS root account credentials to authenticate to AWS. Verification via 'aws sts get-caller-identity' confirmed access as the root account (IAM ARN ending in ':root') of account 684495459427.

With root-level AWS access, the attacker enumerated cloud resources and identified S3 buckets and other storage assets belonging to pcTattletale's infrastructure, including production, dev, and static asset buckets.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: enjoy the loot: pctattle.tar.gz ... pctattletaleapiprod31.tar ... pcttlivereaderprod-bucket-xp252tf4ezzo.tar pctts3logs.tar

attack_chain.description: The attacker downloaded the full contents of multiple S3 buckets, including spyware victim data (screenshots), application code, databases, and logs, bundling them as tar/gz archives.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: enjoy the loot: pctattle.tar.gz manufacture.tar.gz pctattletaleapidev.tar pctattle_app.sql.gz pctattletaleapi.tar WhizPress.tar ... pcttlivereaderprod-bucket-xp252tf4ezzo.tar pctts3logs.tar

attack_chain.description: The attacker defaced pcTattletale's public-facing website, posting a message claiming responsibility for the breach and embedding links to the exfiltrated data files, publicly publishing victim data.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The hacker posted a message on pcTattletale's website late Friday, claiming to have hacked the servers containing pcTattletale's operations. The spyware maker's website briefly had links containing files from its servers, which appeared to include some victims' stolen data.

initial_access_vector: Unauthenticated SOAP API request to pcTattletale's public-facing device registration endpoint, which returned AWS root account credentials in the response body
persistence_mechanism: None identified; the attacker used valid root AWS credentials — no separate persistence mechanism was needed or described
privilege_escalation: AWS root account credentials were returned directly by the vulnerable API; no further escalation was required — the attacker obtained the highest possible privilege level immediately

The hacker posted a message on pcTattletale's website late Friday, claiming to have hacked the servers containing pcTattletale's operations. The spyware maker's website briefly had links containing files from its servers, which appeared to include some victims' stolen data.
